packs.name: audience
packs.description: Who the learner is (children / secondary / undergrad / general public / professional) and the one evidence-based methodology to hand them the concept — CRA, 5E, Peer Instruction, Mayer, Merrill — plus layout defaults. Skill-only — install with `faraday pack add audience`.
packs.quality:
# Pack `audience` — quality bar

- **Audience pinned.** The lesson states who the learner is (or the flagged
  assumption). Building for "everyone" is building for no one.
- **One methodology.** A single audience-appropriate method leads (CRA / 5E / Peer
  Instruction / Mayer / Merrill) — not a blend of all five on every node.
- **Register + density match.** Prose register, symbol density, and which
  interaction carries the load fit the population — not a one-size delivery.
- **Layout fits.** The reading surface (scroll / paged / course / world) follows
  the audience default unless the content or creator overrides it.
- **Not learning-styles.** Method adapts to prior knowledge + motivation, never to
  a claimed visual/auditory "style".

packs.name: exam
packs.description: Turn a topic into a practice test or mock exam — a blueprint-driven set of checks (MCQ, numeric, mission) with honest scoring and review. Skill-only: composes the existing assessment blocks, no new deps. Folder skill with an index that routes to sub-guides.
packs.quality:
# Pack `exam` — quality bar

- **Blueprint-traceable.** Every item maps to a stated outcome with a weight; no
  orphan items, and the mix matches the table of specifications (not all recall).
- **Honest distractors.** Each MCQ wrong option is a nameable misconception, not
  filler; numeric items state units + tolerance.
- **Review that teaches.** The exam ends in a per-outcome result + item review that
  names the misconception and points back into the lesson — not a bare score.
- **Mastery, not curve.** Pass is a criterion (threshold + no outcome floored),
  and the learner can retry missed outcomes.
- **Fair.** No answer/hint leaks during the exam; option/item randomization keeps
  every attempt blueprint-equivalent; timing (if any) is stated and accessible.

packs.name: game2d
packs.description: Educational 2D game stage on PixiJS v8 + @pixi/react — Application host, Matter.js physics, tilemaps, and a Howler audio manager. Author-editable glue copies into src/lesson/game2d. Audience-specific shells (e.g. storybook-game2d) require this pack.
packs.quality:
# Pack `game2d` — quality bar

- **Learning first.** Every interaction teaches or checks an idea. A polished
  arcade loop with no learning outcome fails this pack.
- **React owns structure; Pixi owns frames.** Spawn/despawn with React state;
  continuous motion via `useTick` + refs — never `setState` every frame.
- **Touch + keyboard.** Hit targets are finger-sized when the audience is on a
  tablet; keyboard shortcuts never the only path.
- **Assets are licensed.** Install via **AssetVault** (`node scripts/game2d-assets.mjs starter`).
  Prefer CC0 packs; commit `assetvault.lock.json` when shipping art with the lesson.
- **Cleanup.** Unmount destroys the Pixi app, Matter engine, and Howler sounds —
  no leaked WebGL contexts between slides.
- **Composable.** The stage sits inside a normal Faraday lesson (`Workbench`,
  controls, assessment) — not a full-screen commercial game shell unless a
  specialized pack (e.g. `storybook-game2d`) opts into that frame.

packs.name: lecture-design
packs.description: A folder-ized pedagogy library for lecture/curriculum design — the evidence-based default (backward design, prerequisite graphs, generative learning, spaced retrieval, feed-forward) plus named methodologies (5E, CRA, Peer Instruction, Mayer, Merrill). Skill-only — install with `faraday pack add lecture-design`.
packs.quality:
# Pack `lecture-design` — quality bar

Design-quality rules for lessons/curricula built with this pack. These grade the
*teaching*, not the code.

- **Aligned.** Every lesson and every check maps to an outcome verb; orphan
  content (maps to no outcome) is cut. (backward-design)
- **Gated on mastery.** Progression is gated on demonstrated mastery, not
  exposure; prerequisites are real. (prerequisite-graph)
- **Generative.** The core interaction has the learner predict/construct/explain,
  not just watch or scrub; novices get worked-examples-and-fade. (generative-learning)
- **Retrieval built in.** Units end in a recall check that needs the interaction
  to answer; later nodes re-quiz earlier ideas. (spaced-retrieval)
- **Feed-forward.** Hints say where-to-next, not just right/wrong. (feedback)
- **One methodology.** A single audience-appropriate method leads the lesson; no
  mechanical stacking of all five moves on every node, no learning-styles variants.

packs.name: notes
packs.description: Add a full-page, pressure-sensitive ink canvas for handwriting / sketching lessons on a stylus device. Ships an author-editable <Notebook> component (Canvas + PointerEvents, localStorage-persisted) into src/lesson/notes — no new npm dependencies.
packs.quality:
# Pack `notes` — quality bar

- **Marks are the learning.** Pen input is used because *producing* handwriting /
  sketches / annotations is the point — not as a novelty over a click.
- **Stylus-friendly.** Width follows pressure; the canvas sets `touch-action:
  none` and lives where the page won't scroll-jack under the pen.
- **Stable `notebookId`.** Every `<Notebook>` has a stable id so the learner's ink
  persists across reloads; no volatile or missing ids.
- **Right tool.** `<Notebook>` for an open persistent surface; `<SketchPad>` for a
  graded predict-by-drawing check — not swapped.
- **Checked where it matters.** If an outcome must be assessed, a `<Quiz>` /
  `<Challenge>` gates it — the notebook itself isn't auto-graded.

packs.name: sim2d
packs.description: Educational 2D simulations in a Workbench — SVG diagrams driven by formulas, GSAP timelines, and sim clocks. Replaces the old runtime motion hooks (useRafLoop/useSvgDrag). Author-editable glue copies into src/lesson/sim2d.
packs.quality:
# Pack `sim2d` — quality bar

- **Model first.** Position/velocity come from the lesson's equations — not a physics
  engine fighting the math.
- **SVG + GSAP, not React every frame.** Drive sim time with `useSimTime` /
  `useSimLoop`; update SVG via refs/`gsap.set`. Throttle HUD readouts.
- **Workbench-native.** The stage is a block inside `<Workbench>`, with controls
  in the panel and Play/readouts in `hud`.
- **sim2d vs game2d.** Spatial *explanation* → sim2d. Collisions, sprites,
  tilemaps → `game2d`.
- **Accessible.** SVG `role="img"` + `aria-label` on `<SvgStage>`.

packs.name: slide-view
packs.description: Deliver a lecture as a slide-view presentation — one idea per screen, prev/next, motion. Skill-only: composes the runtime's <SlideDeck> + motion, no new deps. Folder skill with an index that routes to sub-guides.
packs.quality:
# Pack `slide-view` — quality bar

- **One beat per slide.** Each screen carries a single claim/reveal/question/
  interaction; no slide is a paragraph or two ideas fighting for space.
- **Motion reveals.** Every animation builds, transitions, or focuses — nothing
  spins/bounces for flair; `prefers-reduced-motion` degrades cleanly.
- **Still interactive.** Slide view isn't read-only — the learner manipulates and
  predicts across the slides, not just clicks "next".
- **Right format.** Slide view is chosen because the content wants deliberate,
  one-idea-per-screen pacing — not to dress up dense reference material.
- **Self-contained slides.** Because per-slide state resets, each slide makes sense
  on its own when revisited.

packs.name: srs
packs.description: Add a spaced-repetition flashcard deck (SM-2-lite scheduling, localStorage-persisted) for memorization lessons. Ships an author-editable <Flashcards> component into src/lesson/srs — no new npm dependencies.
packs.quality:
# Pack `srs` — quality bar

Additional acceptance rules for lessons that use the `srs` pack.

- **Atomic recall items.** Each card tests one discrete fact (a word, a symbol, a
  date). A card whose answer is a paragraph or a concept is a fail — that content
  wants an interactive, not a flashcard.
- **Retrieval, not recognition.** The reveal-then-grade loop is intact: the
  learner attempts recall before the answer shows. No auto-revealing decks.
- **Stable `deckId`.** Every `<Flashcards>` has a stable `deckId` (the schedule
  persists to it). A missing or volatile id loses the learner's progress.
- **Honest grading path.** All four grades (`Again`/`Hard`/`Good`/`Easy`) are
  reachable; the scheduler is not bypassed or hard-set.
- **Right tool.** Spaced repetition is chosen because the outcome is *memorize/
  recall* — not to drill conceptual understanding that belongs in a sim.

packs.name: stem-methods
packs.description: Fifteen evidence-based teaching methods for math, science, engineering, computing, and statistics — mapped to Faraday block families (not 1:1 blocks). Complements lecture-design (universal moves) and audience (learner population). Skill-only; opt-in via `faraday pack add stem-methods`.
packs.quality:
# Pack `stem-methods` — quality bar

Design-quality rules for STEM lessons built with this pack. These grade the
*discipline fit* of the teaching, not the code.

- **Discipline-fit.** The chosen method matches the content type (procedural,
  conceptual, modeling, design, data, or code) per [selection.md](skill/selection.md).
- **One arc.** A single stem method leads the unit structure; audience chooses
  delivery register; lecture-design moves run underneath — no mechanical stacking
  of every method on every node.
- **Family mapping, not checkbox blocks.** Each phase uses the right *block
  family* (manipulative, formalism, check, etc.) per [block-families.md](skill/block-families.md);
  empty ornament blocks don't count.
- **Misconception-aware (science/math).** Concept checks use documented
  field misconceptions as distractors, not filler wrong answers.
- **Representation match.** Spatial content uses spatial families; processes use
  executable or manipulative families; relationships use data families — matched
  to the *content*, not to a claimed learning style.

packs.name: storybook-game2d
packs.description: Page-turn story shell on top of game2d — fairy-tale pacing, one idea per page, big touch targets, CRA sequencing, visible celebration. Absorbs the former kids tablet preset. Requires game2d (Pixi stage, physics, audio).
packs.quality:
# Pack `storybook-game2d` — quality bar

Inherits `game2d` quality, plus:

- **CRA order.** Concrete (tap/drag in the Pixi page) → picture → symbol last and
  small. Never open a storybook page on bare notation.
- **One idea per page.** `StorybookGame` pages, not a scroll wall; under ~50 words
  of narration per page when aimed at early readers.
- **Finger-sized targets.** Large hit areas inside the stage; drag/tap over typing.
- **Missions, not definitions.** Checks are "do it" moments on a page (or a
  Faraday `<Quiz>` with picture options after the page).
- **Celebrated.** Page-clear and story-end use audio/XP/burst — success is felt.
- **Story serves learning.** Plot dressing is fine; every page still advances an
  idea or checks one.

packs.name: textbook-view
packs.description: Deliver a lecture as a textbook-view presentation — A4-style reading column with scroll, plus free mode (scaled page overview + margin notes). Ships an author-editable <TextbookView> into src/lesson/textbook-view — no new npm dependencies.
packs.quality:
# Pack `textbook-view` — quality bar

- **Denser than slide view.** Pages carry paragraphs and worked steps — not
  one-line captions.
- **Still interactive.** Charts, quizzes, and manipulables belong inside pages;
  textbook view is not a static PDF.
- **Paired intent.** When slide view exists for the same lecture, textbook pages
  expand the same beats — don't fork into a different topic.
- **Free mode notes.** `notesKey` is stable; margin notes persist per page id.

blocks.name: Callout
blocks.summary: <Callout> — a highlighted note (built on Alert).

blocks.name: celebrate
blocks.summary: celebrate(anchor) — a small confetti burst for the moment a check passes. Dep-free canvas overlay: ~90 particles fountain up from the anchor element, colored from the theme's chart tokens, gone in ~1.4s. Respects prefers-reduced-motion (no-op). Blocks call this automatically on success (Quiz/NumericAnswer correct, Challenge clear, SketchPad matched) — authors can also fire it from custom gameplay moments.

blocks.name: Challenge
blocks.summary: <Challenge> — a mission-style PERFORMANCE check: the learner clears it by DOING something in the interactive (hit the target, balance the system, reach the state), not by answering about it. You own the win condition: compute `done` from your sim state and the mission banner + clear state are handled here. The strongest assessment when the outcome verb is "do/tune/ build/achieve" (see assessment.md) — and the most game-like. <Challenge

blocks.name: Chart
blocks.summary: <Chart> — a data chart built on shadcn's ChartContainer (Recharts). Cartesian types (line / bar / area) and radar take rows + an x/angle key + one or more series. Slice types (pie / donut / radial) treat each ROW as a slice: `x` is the slice-name key and the first series' `key` is the value. Series/slice colours default to the theme's --chart-1..5 tokens (light/dark both work). A hover tooltip is on by default; add `legend` for a legend. Rendering is deferred until the container has a real width. Recharts'

blocks.name: CodeCell
blocks.summary: <CodeCell> — an editable, RUNNABLE JavaScript cell (notebook-style). The learner edits the code and presses Run; it executes in a sandboxed iframe (no same-origin access) with console.log/info/warn/error captured into an output panel. Use it when the audience codes or the concept is algorithmic — seeing the numbers come out of your own edit is the interaction. The editor is syntax-highlighted (dep-free tokenizer) via a transparent textarea over a colored layer; the code area shares the card background and

blocks.name: Compare
blocks.summary: <Compare> — tabbed panels for comparing cases side by side (built on Tabs).

blocks.name: CompareModes
blocks.summary: Segmented mode switch driving a side-by-side Compare — contrast two regimes of the same model.

blocks.name: ControlGroup
blocks.summary: <ControlGroup> — a collapsible, labeled section inside a control panel dock. Group related controls semantically (like the sections in a design-tool panel): header shows the label, an optional per-group reset, and a collapse chevron.

blocks.name: Derivation
blocks.summary: <Derivation> — a formula DERIVED live, not decreed. Steps reveal one line at a time (like a teacher at the board): each line is a TeX equation plus the justification for the move ("substitute T", "divide by m", "sin doubles"). The learner paces it with Next step; the newest line slides in highlighted. quality-bar rule: a lesson's central formula must arrive as the LAST line of one of these (or an equivalent stepped walk) — a bare boxed result fails. <Derivation

blocks.name: InkToolbar
blocks.summary: Bottom ink tool strip — icon buttons for pan/draw tools (overview + slide annotate).

blocks.name: Lecture
blocks.summary: <Lecture> — lecture shell: each presentation view owns its full layout. Title and lead flow into slide/textbook content — not a shared header chrome.

blocks.name: Lesson
blocks.summary: <Lesson> — titled header + vertical flow the sections sit in.

blocks.name: McqCheck
blocks.summary: Prose setup plus a multiple-choice gate — recognize the right claim before moving on.

blocks.name: NumericAnswer
blocks.summary: <NumericAnswer> — a free-response check: the learner COMPUTES and types the answer instead of recognizing it in a list. Use when the outcome verb is "calculate/derive/estimate" (see assessment.md); tolerance makes rounding and estimation fair. Enter or Check grades; the hint is feed-forward. <NumericAnswer question="At 24 m/s and 30°, how far does the ball land?" answer={50.9} unit="m" tolerance={1}

blocks.name: NumericGate
blocks.summary: A short derivation followed by a numeric answer check with units and tolerance.

blocks.name: Paged
blocks.summary: @deprecated Use <SlideDeck> from ./SlideDeck — kept for backward compatibility.

blocks.name: ParamLab
blocks.summary: Workbench with parameter controls and a live readout — the default interactive lab shell.

blocks.name: ParamSlider
blocks.summary: <ParamSlider> — a labeled slider with a live value read-out. Exposes a plain number (the underlying Base UI slider is array-capable; we unwrap it).

blocks.name: ParamSwitch
blocks.summary: <ParamSwitch> — a labeled on/off control.

blocks.name: PresentationCanvas
blocks.summary: Free-mode canvas: custom pan/zoom workspace (not React Flow) with positioned content cards and ink drawn on the canvas.

blocks.name: PresentationToolbar
blocks.summary: Hover-reveal toolbar for fullscreen presentation views (slides, textbook). On touch devices the bar stays visible — no hover on tablets.

blocks.name: PresentationTopBar
blocks.summary: Fixed top bar — presentation view tabs (Slides / Textbook) + mode actions.

blocks.name: Prose
blocks.summary: <Prose> — a readable text section, optionally titled.

blocks.name: Quiz
blocks.summary: <Quiz> — a self-check multiple-choice question. Reveals correctness + hint after the learner picks an option and presses Check. Optional callbacks let a lesson react to a pass — e.g. inside a <CourseHost>, wire `onCorrect` to `useNode().complete()` so answering correctly unlocks the next node.

blocks.name: Readout
blocks.summary: <Readout> — a compact label:value chip for LIVE numbers. Use it in a Workbench `hud` overlay (or a control panel) instead of stacking <Stat> cards under a figure — readouts belong ON the instrument, not after it.

blocks.name: Reveal
blocks.summary: <Reveal> — a collapsible hint / spoiler (built on Accordion).

blocks.name: Scrubber
blocks.summary: <Scrubber> — transport controls for a stepped visualization. Wire to useStepper.

blocks.name: Segmented
blocks.summary: <Segmented> — a single-select segmented control (built on Tabs).

blocks.name: SketchPad
blocks.summary: <SketchPad> — a pen/touch drawing check for tablets (Apple Pencil pressure supported): the learner SKETCHES a prediction (a curve, a construction, a region), then reveals the true answer as an overlay and self-assesses. This is the "predict-then-compare" assessment (see assessment.md) — sketches aren't machine-graded; the comparison against the revealed overlay IS the feedback, and the self-assess buttons close the loop. <SketchPad

blocks.name: SlideDeck
blocks.summary: <SlideDeck> — fullscreen slide view: one slide fills the viewport, hover toolbar for navigation, optional overview canvas with ink. The author supplies every slide — including the opening title card (see slide-view skill).

blocks.name: SlideInkLayer
blocks.summary: Per-slide ink overlay — annotate directly on the active slide (present mode).

blocks.name: Stage
blocks.summary: <Stage> — a Card-framed host for a visualization (SVG, canvas, DOM) + caption.

blocks.name: Stat
blocks.summary: <Stat> — a compact metric read-out (built on Card + Badge).

blocks.name: TeX
blocks.summary: <TeX> — KaTeX-rendered math. EVERY symbolic expression in a lesson goes through this block (inline in prose, or `block` for display equations); ASCII/`<code>` math is a quality-bar fail. Pass the TeX source as the child string — String.raw`…` keeps backslashes readable. The area swept obeys <TeX>{String.raw`\frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{L}{2m}`}</TeX>. <TeX block>{String.raw`T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{GM}a^3`}</TeX> <TeX block stream>…</TeX> // writes itself out left→right, professor-style

blocks.name: useLecture
blocks.summary: Shared context for <Lecture> — title/lead and view switching flow into each presentation view instead of a shared header chrome.

blocks.name: Workbench
blocks.summary: <Workbench> — mirror-dimension's workbench layout: a live canvas (center) with a floating control panel (right). Put the visualization in `children` and the controls in `controls` — ideally as <ControlGroup> sections so the panel reads as labeled, collapsible groups. The panel floats (elevated card), sticks while scrolling, and stacks under the canvas on narrow screens. Drop the whole block into the middle of a lesson.
